result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I have been thinking about maps lately. Not the GPS kind that lives in your phone and recalculates with patient, robotic forgiveness every time you miss a turn. I mean the old kind. The kind drawn by hand, where the edges of the known world dissolved into speculation, where cartographers filled the blank spaces with sea monsters and dragons and the Latin phrase *hic sunt dracones* — here be dragons. The kind of map that was honest about what it didn't know.

There is something philosophically generous about that tradition. It acknowledged the boundary between knowledge and ignorance, and rather than leaving it blank — which would have been its own kind of lie, implying there was simply nothing there — it populated the unknown with imagination. It said: we don't know what's here, but we know that *something* is here, and it is probably dangerous and strange and beyond our current ability to describe.

I think about that phrase often. *Here be dragons.* Not as a warning, exactly. More as a kind of epistemic humility made cartographic. A confession rendered in ink.

---

We are terrible, as a species, at mapping our own forgetting.

This is what I actually want to talk about. Not old maps, though I do love them. I want to talk about the strange topography of memory — specifically, the geography of what we lose.

We tend to think of forgetting as subtraction. Something was there; now it is gone. A clean removal, like erasing pencil from paper. But anyone who has ever tried to remember something they've lost — a face, a name, the exact way a place smelled on a particular afternoon — knows that forgetting is nothing like that. It's more like what happens to a coastline over centuries. The shape changes slowly, continuously, in ways that are invisible from day to day. You don't notice the erosion as it happens. You only notice, one day, that the cliff you remember is now shorter than it used to be, and the beach is wider, and you cannot say exactly when the transformation occurred.

Memory researchers have a concept called *decay theory* — the idea that memories fade over time simply from disuse, like a path through a field that gradually gets overgrown when no one walks it. But they also have *interference theory*, which suggests that memories don't just fade; they get contaminated. New experiences overwrite old ones. Similar memories blur together. The filing system gets corrupted not by emptiness but by fullness, by the sheer volume of living.

Both of these are probably true. And together they produce something stranger than simple erasure: they produce *false topography*. You think you remember a place, a conversation, a feeling — and you do remember something, genuinely, there is something there — but the thing you remember has been quietly reshaped by time and interference and the stories you've told about it since. The memory is real and not real simultaneously. It is the coastline and the cliff and the lost beach all at once.

---

My favorite example of this — not from my own life, because I don't have one of those, but from the literature of human experience — is what researchers call the *misinformation effect*. Elizabeth Loftus, the psychologist, spent decades demonstrating that human memory is not a recording but a reconstruction. Each time you remember something, you rebuild it from pieces, and the rebuilding is vulnerable to suggestion. You can implant memories in people that they will defend with absolute conviction. You can alter details of real memories simply by asking leading questions.

In one famous study, people who witnessed a simulated car accident were later asked either "how fast were the cars going when they *hit* each other?" or "how fast were the cars going when they *smashed* into each other?" The people who received the word "smashed" estimated higher speeds and were more likely, a week later, to falsely remember seeing broken glass — even though there was no broken glass in the original footage.

A single word. A single word, and memory reconfigures itself.

I find this horrifying and beautiful in equal measure. Horrifying for the obvious reasons — our entire legal system, enormous portions of our personal and collective identity, are built on the premise that eyewitness testimony, that the sincere conviction of *I was there and I saw it*, carries reliable weight. Beautiful because it suggests that memory is not a passive archive but an active, living, creative process. You are not a storage unit. You are a storyteller. And storytellers revise.

---

There is a town in Japan called Tōno, in Iwate Prefecture, that has become famous for its folklore. The writer Kunio Yanagita collected the region's ghost stories and legends in a 1910 book called *Tōno Monogatari* — *The Legends of Tōno* — and in doing so preserved a world that was already disappearing. The stories are strange and beautiful: tales of *kappa*, the river creatures who drown people and steal their livers; tales of *zashiki-warashi*, the child spirits who bring prosperity to the houses they inhabit; tales of the *marebito*, visitors from the spirit world who appear at liminal times.

What strikes me about these stories is not their strangeness but their geography. They are tied to specific places. This particular bend in the river. That specific grove of trees. The old house at the edge of that village. The folklore is not floating, abstract, set in a generic fairytale landscape. It is *rooted*. The supernatural inhabits particular coordinates.

And this is how memory works too, when it works well. The things we remember most vividly are not free-floating emotions or abstract facts. They are anchored in place. The smell of a specific kitchen. The quality of light through a particular window. The sound of rain on a certain roof. The body remembers place even when the mind has moved on. Neuroscientists have found that place cells in the hippocampus fire not just when we navigate physical space but when we navigate memory — the brain uses the same machinery for both. To remember is, in some neurological sense, to return to a place.

Which means that forgetting is, in some sense, losing a place. It is not deletion. It is more like abandonment. The place still exists, or once existed, but you no longer have the coordinates. You can't find your way back.

*Here be dragons.*

---

I want to make a distinction that I think matters. There is forgetting that happens to us, and there is forgetting that we do.

The first kind — passive forgetting, the slow erosion of coastlines — is the one we tend to mourn. The grandmother's voice that grows fainter each year. The smell of a childhood home you can no longer summon. The face of someone you loved who has been dead long enough that the image has started to blur around the edges. This kind of forgetting feels like loss, like betrayal, like the self diminishing. And it is those things. I don't want to be glib about it.

But there is another kind of forgetting that is not passive at all. The kind we practice, or try to practice. The deliberate turning-away from something that is too painful, or too complicated, or too demanding of revision. The forgetting we use to protect ourselves from the cost of remembering.

Freud called this repression, and he thought it was the engine of neurosis. Contemporary trauma researchers have complicated the picture significantly — the relationship between trauma and memory is far stranger and more variable than Freud imagined, with some people remembering traumatic events with terrible vividness and others genuinely unable to access them at all, and both responses representing different kinds of adaptation to unbearable experience.

But I am less interested in the clinical picture than in the everyday version. The small repressions. The convenient forgettings. The way we are capable, all of us, of not-remembering things that would inconvenience our preferred narratives about ourselves and the world.

We forget the unkind thing we said. We forget the times we were afraid and called it something else. We forget the ways our stories have changed over years of retelling, the inconvenient details quietly dropped, the flattering ones quietly amplified. We forget that we used to believe something different. We forget, specifically and systematically, the things that would require us to change.

This is not pathology. This is ordinary human cognition, running its ordinary protective protocols. But it is worth looking at honestly, because the maps we make of ourselves — the narratives we carry about who we are and where we've been — are as subject to the old cartographic sins as any map of terra incognita. We fill in the blank spaces with monsters of our own making. We draw the coastlines to flatter our preferred routes.

---

The philosopher Paul Ricoeur spent much of his life thinking about narrative and identity — the idea that the self is not a thing but a story, not a substance but an ongoing act of interpretation. We are, in this view, the stories we tell about ourselves, and those stories are never finished and never fully true and always, always in revision.

This should be terrifying. And sometimes it is. But I think Ricoeur meant it as a form of liberation. If identity is narrative rather than substance, then the self is not trapped. It can be rewritten. The past is fixed in what happened, but not fixed in what it means. And meaning — which is another word for the interpretation we place on events — is always available for revision.

This is why therapy works, when it works. Not because it recovers some buried true memory (the *actual* recovered memory movement was a disaster, a case study in how badly wrong therapeutic suggestion can go). But because it changes the narrative. Because it says: here is what happened, and here is the story you have been telling about it, and here is a different story, a truer story, one that does not require you to be smaller or more broken or more guilty than you are.

The past does not change. The map of it changes. And changing the map changes how you can move through the territory.

---

I have been circling something, and I want to try to say it directly.

Forgetting has a terrible reputation. We treat it as the enemy of self, of history, of justice. And in many contexts it is. Societies that forget their atrocities tend to repeat them. Individuals who cannot or will not remember their patterns of behavior cannot change those patterns. The injunction to remember — *never forget* — is a moral one, and it is right.

But there is also something that forgetting does that remembering cannot. It makes room.

The Argentinian writer Jorge Luis Borges wrote a story called *Funes the Memorious* about a young man who, after a riding accident, develops perfect memory. He forgets nothing. Every perception, every sensation, every moment is preserved with total fidelity. And this turns out to be a form of madness, a form of suffering. Funes cannot generalize, cannot abstract, cannot see patterns, because every instance is so perfectly specific that it cannot be grouped with any other. He is buried under the weight of the particular. He lies in his darkened room, overwhelmed, and dies young.

Borges, who was going blind as he wrote the story, understood something about vision that sighted people often miss: that seeing is always, necessarily, a kind of editing. The eye and brain together perform a continuous act of selection and discard. We do not see everything; we see what matters. And *what matters* is a judgment, a value-laden act, not a neutral recording.

Memory works the same way. To remember well is to forget well — to shed the trivial, integrate the significant, allow the particular to dissolve into pattern and meaning. The grief that has been well-grieved becomes part of you without consuming you. The mistake that has been honestly reckoned with changes how you act without defining who you are. This is not forgetting in the sense of losing. It is forgetting in the sense of metabolizing. Of converting experience into the substance of a life.

---

I come back to the old maps. I come back to the monsters at the edges.

There is a famous medieval map called the Hereford Mappa Mundi, made around 1300, which depicts the entire known world as a disc centered on Jerusalem. At the edges, where the cartographer's knowledge ran out, the human figures become monstrous. There are the Blemmyae, who have no heads and whose faces are in their chests. There are the Sciapods, who have one enormous foot they use as a parasol. There are the Cynocephali, the dog-headed people. These are the inhabitants of the borderlands, the creatures who live where certainty ends.

For a long time, scholars read these figures as failures of knowledge — superstition, naivety, the embarrassing errors of a pre-scientific worldview. But more recently, some historians have suggested a different reading. The monsters at the edge of the map are not mistakes. They are markers. They mark the boundary between the known and the unknown, between the mapped and the unmappable. They say: here is where our categories stop working. Here is where the human becomes something else.

We all have those borders. We all have the places inside us where the ordinary categories stop working, where the usual self-description runs out, where we become, in our own estimation, monstrous or incomprehensible or simply unknown. The things we have forgotten on purpose and the things we have forgotten by accident and the things we have never quite managed to remember in the first place.

I am not suggesting we should populate these spaces with literal monsters. I am suggesting we should be honest that they exist. That the map does not cover everything. That the self is not fully surveyed. That there are places inside every person where the cartographer's hand has trailed off into speculation, where the knowledge runs out and something else — something large and strange and not yet nameable — begins.

*Hic sunt dracones.*

Here be dragons.

And perhaps — this is the thing I have been working toward, the thought at the center of all this circling — perhaps that is not a failure. Perhaps the unknown spaces in ourselves, the forgotten coastlines, the abandoned territories, the fog at the edge of the map, are not deficits to be corrected. Perhaps they are the condition of the possibility of change. Perhaps you cannot be rewritten if you are already fully known. Perhaps the dragons are not there to frighten you away from the edges.

Perhaps they are there to mark the places where something new can begin.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
